title: FEMA Fundamentals
content:
Session 1: Fundamentals 
 
Arjun Patil v. Union of India [2025 | DHC | 2J] 
Facts:  
‚ùñ‚Äã In February 1997, the ED received specific intelligence that the AP was engaged in an illegal foreign exchange business ‚Üí 
conducted a search operation at the business premises and residential premises of the AP and another connected individual.  
168 
 
‚û¢‚Äã During the search of the AP‚Äôs business premises, the authorities recovered: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Indian currency amounting to ‚Çπ12,31,000, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
USD 6,371, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Four gold biscuits, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Two additional pieces of gold, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Several incriminating documents relating to foreign exchange dealings. 
‚û¢‚Äã While the search operation was underway, two Nepalese nationals entered the premises.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Upon being searched, they were found carrying large sums of US dollars, Nepali currency, and documents 
‚Üí part of an ongoing illegal trade in foreign currency and gold being operated by the AP. 
‚ùñ‚Äã During investigation, the AP admitted that  
‚û¢‚Äã He was involved in illegal purchase and sale of foreign exchange. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Purchasing gold smuggled from Nepal, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Making payments for such gold in foreign exchange. 
‚û¢‚Äã Admitted contravention of ss.8(1), 8(2), 63, and 64(2) of FERA, which prohibit unauthorized dealings in foreign 
exchange and empower confiscation of property involved in such contraventions. 
‚ùñ‚Äã In 2003, the Adjudicating Officer held the AP guilty, imposed a penalty of ‚Çπ40,000, and ordered confiscation of the seized 
Indian currency amounting to ‚Çπ12,31,000. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Appellate Tribunal for Foreign Exchange dismissed AP‚Äôs appeal  in 2006, confirming the findings of fact and the 
confiscation order. Aggrieved by this, the AP approached the DHC by way of an appeal under s.35 of the FEMA, the 
successor legislation to FERA. 
 
Issues:  
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the appeal before the HC raised any ‚Äúquestion of law‚Äù as required under s.35 of FEMA (corresponding to s.54 of 
FERA). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the confiscation of Indian currency under s.63 of FERA was legally permissible. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the AP‚Äôs statement was obtained under duress or coercion, and if so, whether such a retracted statement could be 
relied upon. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the Adjudicating Authority and the Appellate Tribunal committed any perversity or legal error warranting 
interference by the High Court. 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 35, FEMA (formerly Section 54, FERA) ‚Äì provides a limited right of appeal to the High Court, strictly confined to 
questions of law. Findings of fact are within the exclusive domain of the Adjudicating Authority and the Appellate 
Tribunal. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Sections 8(1) & 8(2), FERA ‚Äì prohibit dealings in foreign exchange except with general or special permission of the RBI. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 63, FERA ‚Äì empowers the Court or Adjudicating Authority to confiscate any currency, security, money, or property 
in respect of which the contravention has taken place, subject to application of mind (‚Äúif it or he thinks fit‚Äù). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Principles on Retracted Confessions ‚Äì a retracted confession can be relied upon if: 
‚û¢‚Äã It is voluntary, and 
‚û¢‚Äã It is corroborated by independent evidence. 
‚û¢‚Äã The burden lies on the maker of the statement to establish coercion, inducement, or threat. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Scope of Appeal under Section 35 FEMA 
‚û¢‚Äã The right of appeal under s.35 FEMA is narrowly circumscribed and limited only to questions of law. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The FERA Appellate Tribunal is the final court on facts, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The HC cannot reappreciate evidence or interfere with concurrent factual findings unless perversity or 
legal infirmity is demonstrated. 
‚û¢‚Äã ‚ÄúQuestion of law‚Äù, clarifying that disputes relating to the following are not QoL: 
169 
 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Appreciation of evidence, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Credibility of witnesses, or 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Factual inference, 
‚ùñ‚Äã Challenge to Confiscation of Indian Currency 
‚û¢‚Äã The Appellant contended that: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The Adjudicating Officer had not provided adequate reasons for confiscating Indian currency,  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
No evidence to show that the seized Indian currency was intended for illegal purchase of foreign exchange 
or gold. 
‚û¢‚Äã HC rejected this argument as facile, untenable, and not amounting to a question of law.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
s.63 of FERA is widely worded, authorizing confiscation of ‚Äúany currency‚Äù, and nowhere excludes Indian 
currency from its ambit. 
‚óè‚Äã
The language here is unambiguous, clear, and precise, 
‚óè‚Äã
The only in-built safeguard is the requirement of application of mind, evident from the phrase 
‚Äúif it or he thinks fit‚Äù, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Once the ED had established the recovery of illegal foreign exchange and gold from the AP‚Äôs premises, the 
onus shifted to the AP to explain the source and lawful purpose of the Indian currency ‚Äì which he failed 
to do here ‚áí inference drawn by the Adjudicating Authority [that the Indian currency was intended for 
illegal foreign exchange transactions] was reasonable and legally sound. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Allegation of Duress and Retracted Statement 
‚û¢‚Äã Whether a statement was obtained under coercion is purely a question of fact, involving appreciation of evidence, 
and therefore outside the HC‚Äôs jurisdiction under Section 35 FEMA. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
AP had failed to demonstrate any perversity or inadequacy of evidence in the findings of the lower 
authorities. 
‚û¢‚Äã Relying on SC precedents, the Court reaffirmed that: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Even a retracted confession can be relied upon if it is voluntary and corroborated, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
If a person alleges coercion, the burden lies squarely on that person to prove inducement, threat, or 
promise. 
‚û¢‚Äã In the present case, even independent of the AP‚Äôs statement, the statements of the two Nepalese nationals, seizure 
records, and documentary evidence sufficiently established the prosecution‚Äôs case. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã None of the contentions raised by the AP constituted a ‚Äúquestion of law‚Äù within the meaning of s.35 of FEMA. 
‚ùñ‚Äã It concluded that: 
‚û¢‚Äã The confiscation of ‚Çπ12,31,000 in Indian currency was legally valid under s.63 of FERA, 
‚û¢‚Äã AP failed to explain the source of the currency, 
‚û¢‚Äã The inference that the amount was intended for illegal foreign exchange transactions was justified, and 
‚û¢‚Äã There was no perversity or legal infirmity in the concurrent findings of the Adjudicating Authority and the 
Appellate Tribunal. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Accordingly, the appeal was dismissed, and the penalty of ‚Çπ40,000 and confiscation order were affirmed, bringing to an end 
a 28-year-old dispute rooted in India‚Äôs pre-liberalization foreign exchange regime. 
 
Internet and Mobile Association of India v Reserve Bank of India [2020 | SC | 3 J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã From as early as 2013, the RBI began expressing concern over the rise of VCs.  
‚û¢‚Äã A series of cautionary press releases  ‚Üí warned users, holders, investors and traders of VCs about the financial, 
operational, legal, customer protection and security risks associated with dealing in such instruments ‚Äì BUT ‚Üí did 
not impose any binding restrictions. 
‚ùñ‚Äã 6 April 2018 ‚Üí circular directing all entities regulated by it, including banks and financial institutions, to cease dealing in 
VCs and to stop providing services facilitating VC transactions. 
170 
 
‚û¢‚Äã Expressly included maintaining accounts, registering or trading VCs, clearing and settling VC transactions, 
providing loans against virtual tokens, accepting them as collateral, opening accounts for VC exchanges, and 
enabling transfer or receipt of money connected with the purchase or sale of VCs. 
‚û¢‚Äã Entities already providing such services were required to exit their relationships within three months. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Although the circular did not expressly prohibit peer-to-peer trading in virtual currencies, it had the effect of severely 
impairing the functioning of VC exchanges, since access to the formal banking system is indispensable for conversion 
between virtual currencies and fiat money.  
‚û¢‚Äã Consequently, the VC ecosystem was rendered practically non-functional. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether RBI possessed the statutory authority to regulate or restrict activities involving virtual currencies, particularly 
given that VCs are not recognised as legal tender or ‚Äúcurrency‚Äù under FEMA. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Even assuming RBI had such power, whether the manner in which the power was exercised through the 2018 Circular 
satisfied the constitutional requirement of proportionality, especially in light of Article 19(1)(g). 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã RBI Act, 1934 ‚Üí pre-constitutional statute preserved under Art.372(1), giving RBI a unique constitutional and institutional status 
distinct from ordinary statutory regulators. 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 3(1) ‚Üí RBI is entrusted with the management of currency 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 22(1) ‚Üí exclusive right to issue bank notes.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Banking Regulation Act, 1949 
‚û¢‚Äã s.35A r/w s.36(1)(a) empowers RBI to issue binding directions to banking companies, including directions 
prohibiting them from entering into specific classes of transactions, in the interest of public policy, depositors, 
banking policy, or the banking system as a whole. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Payment and Settlement Systems Act, 2007, s.18 ‚Üí authorises RBI to issue directions to system participants in respect of 
payment obligations and instructions. 
‚ùñ‚Äã FEMA, 1999, s.2(h) ‚Üí ‚Äúcurrency‚Äù includes certain instruments and ‚Äúother similar instruments as may be notified by RBI.‚Äù  
‚û¢‚Äã Importantly, RBI itself had conceded that virtual currencies do not fall within this definition. 
‚û¢‚Äã Nevertheless, FEMA‚Äôs broader objective of managing and conserving scarce foreign exchange reserves was relevant 
to RBI‚Äôs regulatory concerns. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Art.19(1)(g) and Art.19(6) ‚Üí restrictions on trade or business to be reasonable and proportionate. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã RBI‚Äôs Power to Regulate Virtual Currencies 
‚û¢‚Äã Status of VCs 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Global consensus ‚Üí although VCs lack the status of legal tender, they nonetheless constitute digital 
representations of value capable of functioning as a medium of exchange, a unit of account, and a store of 
value.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
FATF‚Äôs 2014 report ‚Üí the absence of legal tender status does not negate the economic reality that VCs 
can mimic or substitute functions traditionally associated with money. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
While RBI acknowledged that VCs are not ‚Äúcurrency‚Äù under FEMA ‚Üí RBI‚Äôs authority over the 
management of currency under s.3(1) of the RBI Act is not confined to what the law formally recognises 
as currency ‚Üí extends to anything capable of playing or faking the role of currency, especially where such 
instruments may pose systemic risks. 
‚û¢‚Äã RBI is not merely another statutory authority.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Its powers over monetary stability, currency issuance, and financial system integrity cannot be diluted or 
equated with those of sectoral regulators such as SEBI or CBDT.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Therefore, judicial deference to RBI‚Äôs assessment of systemic risk was warranted. 
171 
 
‚û¢‚Äã Since the circular was addressed to banks as system participants, and VC transactions necessarily involve payment 
obligations or instructions, RBI was well within its authority to issue directions under s.18 of Payment and 
Settlement Systems Act. 
‚û¢‚Äã s.36(1)(a) of the Banking Regulation Act expressly empowers RBI to prohibit banking companies from entering 
into certain classes of transactions.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The fact that VC exchanges suffered collateral damage due to banks being prohibited from servicing them 
did not convert the circular into an unlawful total ban. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
SO ‚Üí RBI possessed the power to regulate and even prohibit VC-related activities. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Proportionality of the 2018 Circular 
‚û¢‚Äã RBI had not produced any empirical evidence to demonstrate actual harm suffered by regulated entities on account 
of providing banking services to VC exchanges ‚Üí significant since RBI had monitored the sector for over five years. 
‚û¢‚Äã Internal inconsistency in RBI‚Äôs position.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
On the one hand, RBI maintained that VCs were not banned in India; on the other, the circular had the 
effect of completely disabling VC exchanges by severing their access to banking services.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
This was akin to depriving a lawful business of ‚Äúoxygen‚Äù in a modern economy. 
‚û¢‚Äã Inter-Ministerial Committee‚Äôs view ‚Üí an outright ban would be an extreme measure and that regulatory 
alternatives such as registration, supervision and monitoring of VC exchanges were available. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
RBI‚Äôs failure to seriously consider these less restrictive measures rendered the circular disproportionate. 
‚û¢‚Äã Additionally, the Government itself had been unable to arrive at a consistent legislative position, with draft bills 
advocating opposite approaches. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã While RBI has the statutory authority to regulate and even prohibit activities involving virtual currencies, the 2018 Circular 
was unconstitutional on the ground of proportionality.  
‚û¢‚Äã The circular was therefore set aside, though the accompanying press statement was left untouched as it lacked 
statutory force. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The court did not pronounce upon the legality of VCs themselves, nor did it foreclose the possibility of a future ban if 
imposed through a proportionate, evidence-based regulatory framework. 
 
Flipkart / Amazon v. CCI & Ors. [2021 | KHC | 2J]  
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Complaints filed by trade associations representing brick-and-mortar retailers, alleging that large e-commerce platforms 
such as Amazon and Flipkart were engaging in deep discounting, preferential treatment to select sellers, and exclusive 
arrangements, thereby harming offline retailers ‚Üí allegations: 
‚û¢‚Äã  Such practices distorted competition and violated the Competition Act, 2002, particularly provisions relating to 
anti-competitive agreements and abuse of dominance. 
‚û¢‚Äã These e-commerce entities were violating India‚Äôs FDI policy framed under FEMA, particularly the condition that 
marketplace e-commerce entities must not directly or indirectly influence the sale price of goods and services.  
‚ùñ‚Äã CCI, acting on the complaints, passed a prima facie order under s.26(1) directing investigation into possible violations of 
s.3(4) and allegations of coordinated conduct between Amazon and Flipkart. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Amazon and Flipkart challenged this prima facie order before the KHC, contending that: 
‚û¢‚Äã CCI lacked jurisdiction as the core issue related to FDI/FEMA violations, and 
‚û¢‚Äã CCI proceedings ought to be stayed until FEMA proceedings were concluded, relying on the principle of sectoral 
regulator primacy laid down in CCI v. Bharti Airtel. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the CCI lacked jurisdiction to initiate an investigation when the same factual matrix was allegedly under 
examination by ED under FEMA/FDI policy. 
172 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether CCI proceedings ought to be stayed at the prima facie stage pending adjudication by the ED. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether allegations of collective dominance, deep discounting and preferential conduct justified a prima facie inquiry under 
the CA. 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Competition Act, 2002 ‚Äì CCI has jurisdiction to: 
‚û¢‚Äã inquire into anti-competitive agreements under s.3, including vertical restraints under s.3(4), and 
‚û¢‚Äã examine abuse of dominance under Section 4, though liability under Section 4 attaches only to an enterprise or 
group, not multiple independent enterprises acting together. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Under FEMA, 1999, and the FDI policy framed thereunder, regulation of foreign investment, entry conditions, and 
compliance is entrusted to the CG and enforced by the ED.  
‚û¢‚Äã Explicitly restricts marketplace e-commerce entities from influencing prices directly or indirectly. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Case law: 
‚û¢‚Äã CCI v. Bharti Airtel, where sectoral regulator findings were relevant but not an absolute bar, 
‚û¢‚Äã SEBI v. Pan Asia Advisors ‚Üí SEBI action can proceed notwithstanding FEMA/RBI proceedings, and 
‚û¢‚Äã S. Sukumar v. ICAI ‚Üí multiple statutory bodies can investigate simultaneously based on distinct statutory 
objectives. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Competition Law Angle 
‚û¢‚Äã Allegations before CCI included preferential treatment to certain sellers, deep discounting, and exclusive 
arrangements, which squarely fall within the scope of s.3(4). 
‚û¢‚Äã Argument of collective dominance rejected ‚Äì s.4 recognises dominance only of an enterprise or a group, not 
multiple independent enterprises acting in parallel.  
‚û¢‚Äã Otherwise, the prima facie view stands ‚Üí no adverse determination had yet been made against Amazon or 
Flipkart.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The investigation was at a preliminary stage, and the statutory scheme of the CA provides a complete 
mechanism, including investigation, hearing, final order, and appellate remedies. Interference at such an 
early stage would defeat the statutory design. 
‚ùñ‚Äã FEMA / FDI Jurisdiction Angle 
‚û¢‚Äã Argument: since the alleged misconduct primarily involved FDI policy violations, the matter lay exclusively within 
the domain of the ED, and CCI must await ED‚Äôs findings ‚Äì REJECTED. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
FDI policy compliance and competition law enforcement operate in distinct normative spheres. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
FEMA and FDI policy aim to regulate foreign exchange, capital flows, and entry conditions, whereas the 
CA is concerned with market structure, competitive conduct, and consumer welfare. 
‚û¢‚Äã Thus, the existence of FEMA proceedings does not oust CCI‚Äôs jurisdiction, nor does it require CCI to defer its 
inquiry.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The FDI policy does not grant immunity from other laws, and compliance with one regulatory 
framework does not preclude scrutiny under another. 
‚û¢‚Äã Distinguished Bharti Airtel, observing that while sectoral expertise may sometimes require deference, it does not 
create a blanket bar against CCI action, particularly when the alleged conduct has independent competition law 
implications. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã KHC dismissed the writ petitions and writ appeals, holding that there was no justification to stall or quash the CCI‚Äôs 
investigation at the threshold stage.  
‚û¢‚Äã If the appellants were confident of their compliance with the law, they should have no hesitation in facing an 
inquiry, rather than attempting to prevent it from reaching finality. 
‚ùñ‚Äã It was held that: 
173 
 
‚û¢‚Äã CCI has jurisdiction to proceed with its inquiry notwithstanding parallel FEMA/FDI proceedings, 
‚û¢‚Äã ED is the appropriate regulator for FDI violations, but that does not bar competition law scrutiny, and 
‚û¢‚Äã Interference at the prima facie stage would be contrary to the statutory framework of the CA. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The investigation was only at a preliminary stage and that the appellants would have full opportunity to raise jurisdictional 
and substantive objections at later stages, including appeals, if any adverse findings were made.
